findings.description: verify_checksum() slurps the entire stored file into memory with f.read() before hashing, so a 200MB upload pins ~200MB+ RAM per call (doubled while hashlib.sha256(content) holds a second reference) and N concurrent verifications multiply it into OOM territory; stream the file in fixed-size blocks into hashlib instead (h=hashlib.sha256(); for block in iter(lambda: f.read(1<<20), b''): h.update(block)) for O(1) memory.

The MAX_FILE_MB gate runs in a separate file_size_ok() call only after assemble() has already concatenated every chunk to disk and after verify_checksum() may have read the whole file into memory, so the size limit never actually bounds the line-83 memory load — the expensive full-file read happens before anything checks the size, making the memory cost effectively unbounded regardless of MAX_FILE_MB (DoS framing deferred to critic-security).

Off-by-one: range(session.total_chunks - 1) iterates indices 0..total_chunks-2 and silently drops the final chunk, producing a truncated/corrupt assembled file (should be range(session.total_chunks)) — logic bug, deferred to critic-logic; perf-adjacent only in that the subsequent verify_checksum read is wasted on a guaranteed-mismatching file.

self._sessions and session.received are mutated without any locking across start/receive_chunk/assemble/abort; in a multi-threaded web service concurrent requests for the same upload_id race (lost chunk writes, dict mutation during iteration), and del/pop on line 70/95 can KeyError under interleaving — thread-safety concern deferred to critic-logic, noted because the module is described as web-service code.
